izandla ziyagezana.idiomatic: Human beings are mutually dependent; cooperation and community support ensure survival.
izandla ziyagezana.context: A foundational linguistic pillar of Ubuntu. It highlights the customary law of reciprocity, implying that rendering aid to another ensures that you too will receive aid when your resources fail.
izandla ziyagezana.source: Verified by Council Review — Council Elder G. Khanyile

kgomo e bolawa ke molapo.literal: A cow is killed by a river/stream.
kgomo e bolawa ke molapo.idiomatic: A person can easily be undone or destroyed by the very thing that sustains or nurtures them.
kgomo e bolawa ke molapo.context: A profound warning against complacency. Just as livestock must go to the valley stream to survive but run the risk of drowning, humans must remain cautious around their primary sources of wealth, power, or comfort.
kgomo e bolawa ke molapo.source: Verified by Council Review — M. Khumalo (Heritage Specialist)

ukhuni ludala luza nentshulube.idiomatic: Old age comes with its burdens, frailties, or vulnerabilities.
ukhuni ludala luza nentshulube.context: A deeply philosophical expression used by elders to denote the natural, unavoidable physical and mental challenges that accompany aging, spoken with resigned wisdom rather than bitterness.
ukhuni ludala luza nentshulube.source: Verified by Council Review — Dr. L. Ndlovu (UNISA Linguistics Dept.)

lefeela le kgotso le phala nala e nang l.phrase: Lefeela le kgotso le phala nala e nang le dhabo
lefeela le kgotso le phala nala e nang l.literal: Nothingness with peace is better than abundance with trouble.
lefeela le kgotso le phala nala e nang l.idiomatic: Peace of mind and tranquil stability are infinitely more valuable than material wealth gained through conflict or anxiety.
lefeela le kgotso le phala nala e nang l.context: A cornerstone proverb in Sotho governance philosophies, heavily emphasized in traditional court assemblies (Lekgotla) to de-escalate civil greed and prioritize communal harmony over material disputes.
lefeela le kgotso le phala nala e nang l.source: Verified by Council Review — Mme T. Mokoena (Sotho Cultural Custodian)

muvhuda wa mbevha a u na mutsha.phrase: Muvhuda wa mbevha a u na mutsha
muvhuda wa mbevha a u na mutsha.literal: The track made by a mouse does not last until morning.
muvhuda wa mbevha a u na mutsha.idiomatic: Deception, lies, or secret malicious actions will quickly be exposed by the light of truth.
muvhuda wa mbevha a u na mutsha.context: Used historically by Vhavenda traditional leadership to warn individuals against practicing hidden witchcraft, theft, or sabotage within the village, reminding them that nature inevitably reveals hidden deeds.
muvhuda wa mbevha a u na mutsha.source: Verified by Council Review — Chief V. Ramabulana (Venda Traditional Council)

ntsu kulu yi vhona hi ku thonya.phrase: Ntsu kulu yi vhona hi ku thonya
ntsu kulu yi vhona hi ku thonya.literal: A great eagle is recognized by its stoop/dive.
ntsu kulu yi vhona hi ku thonya.idiomatic: True leadership, power, or capability is demonstrated through actions and decisive execution, not empty boasting.
ntsu kulu yi vhona hi ku thonya.context: A Tsonga honorific idiom often used to praise an individual who speaks very little but delivers outstanding results for the community during times of crisis or structural need.
ntsu kulu yi vhona hi ku thonya.source: Verified by Council Review — H. Baloyi (Tsonga Oral Historian)
